# Background

## The systematic review bottleneck

Systematic reviews occupy the apex of the evidence hierarchy, providing the most rigorous synthesis of research findings to inform clinical decision-making, health technology assessment, and guideline development. Organizations such as the Cochrane Collaboration, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, and the World Health Organization rely on systematic reviews as the evidentiary backbone for policy recommendations. Yet the very rigor that confers this status also imposes an extraordinary burden on research teams. An analysis of the PROSPERO registry found that the median time from registration to publication of a systematic review is 67.3 weeks, with some reviews requiring well over two years to complete [@Borah2017Analysis]. During this protracted timeline, new primary studies accumulate, potentially rendering conclusions outdated before they reach print.

The scale of this challenge is accelerating. PubMed now indexes over three million articles annually, and the biomedical literature doubles approximately every seven years. For review teams conducting comprehensive searches across multiple databases, the number of records requiring screening routinely reaches into the thousands or tens of thousands. In hematology and oncology alone, the rapid proliferation of novel therapeutics---including bispecific antibodies, antibody-drug conjugates, and CAR-T cell therapies---has generated a surge of clinical trial publications that outpaces the capacity of traditional review methods. The consequence is a widening gap between the evidence that exists and the evidence that has been synthesized, precisely when clinicians, payers, and regulators need timely guidance most.

## Current automation landscape

Recognizing this bottleneck, the past decade has seen the emergence of software tools designed to automate discrete stages of the systematic review workflow. ASReview, an open-source active learning platform, applies machine learning to prioritize records during title-and-abstract screening, substantially reducing the number of irrelevant records that human reviewers must evaluate [@vandeSchoot2021ASReview]. Rayyan provides a collaborative web interface augmented with semi-automated AI-assisted screening capabilities [@Ouzzani2016Rayyan]. Covidence offers a managed platform that streamlines screening, data extraction, and quality assessment through structured forms and deduplication workflows [@Covidence2024]. For data extraction and evidence mapping, Elicit leverages large language models (LLMs) to parse full-text articles and populate structured extraction tables [@Elicit2024]. On the synthesis and writing side, LitLLM demonstrated that LLMs can generate draft literature review text from retrieved references [@LitLLM2024].

More recently, otto-SR combined LLM capabilities across multiple review stages, achieving 96.7% sensitivity in automated screening and 93.1% accuracy in structured data extraction [@ottoSR2025]. These results suggest that LLM-based automation is approaching human-level performance on individual tasks. Two landmark reviews in the *Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association* have mapped the emerging role of LLMs across the review lifecycle, documenting rapid adoption alongside persistent concerns regarding hallucination, reproducibility, and validation [@JAMIA2025LLMemergence; @JAMIA2025EnhancingSLR]. Concurrently, two survey articles published in *Systematic Reviews* provided comprehensive assessments of the AI-assisted review landscape, cataloguing available tools and identifying methodological gaps [@Ge2024Leveraging; @Khalil2024Automation].

Despite this progress, a critical gap remains. Existing tools address isolated stages---screening, extraction, or narrative generation---but none provides an integrated, end-to-end pipeline that bridges from literature search through to a submission-ready manuscript with full PRISMA 2020 compliance [@Page2021PRISMA]. The handoffs between tools introduce friction, inconsistency, and opportunities for error. What is needed is a unified system that orchestrates the entire workflow while maintaining the methodological transparency that systematic reviews demand.

## LLM engineering as an emerging discipline

The technical foundations for such a system have matured rapidly. Prompt engineering has evolved from ad-hoc trial-and-error into a systematic discipline, with comprehensive taxonomies cataloguing dozens of established techniques and their empirical effectiveness [@Schulhoff2024PromptReport; @Sahoo2024Systematic]. Several advances are particularly relevant to evidence synthesis. Multi-agent orchestration decomposes complex tasks across specialized LLM agents that collaborate through structured communication protocols, enabling divide-and-conquer strategies for workflows that exceed the capacity of a single model call. Structured output prompting constrains LLM responses to conform to predefined schemas---such as JSON extraction templates or PRISMA checklist items---ensuring machine-readable, auditable outputs. The LLM-as-judge paradigm, in which one model instance evaluates the output of another against explicit criteria, provides a scalable mechanism for automated quality assurance [@Zheng2024Judging]. Reflexion and self-repair loops enable agents to critique their own outputs and iteratively refine them, reducing error propagation across pipeline stages [@Shinn2023Reflexion]. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) grounds LLM outputs in source documents, mitigating hallucination by anchoring generated text to retrieved evidence [@Lewis2020RAG].

Each of these techniques has been validated in isolation across diverse domains, yet they have not been systematically composed and applied to the structured, multi-stage workflow of systematic evidence synthesis. The opportunity to integrate these methods into a coherent pipeline---purpose-built for the demands of systematic reviews---remains unexplored.

## Objectives

In this paper, we present Robust-Lit-Review, an end-to-end LLM-engineered pipeline that automates the systematic review workflow from database search through submission-ready manuscript generation. Our objectives are fivefold: (1) to describe the design and architecture of the pipeline, including its multi-agent orchestration, structured prompting strategies, and quality assurance mechanisms; (2) to validate pipeline performance across three hematology/oncology case studies spanning distinct clinical questions and evidence landscapes; (3) to compare pipeline-generated reviews against independently published expert reviews on concordance of included studies, extracted data, and clinical conclusions; (4) to evaluate the degree of PRISMA 2020 reporting compliance achieved by automated manuscript generation; and (5) to assess the role of human-in-the-loop checkpoints at critical decision points, characterizing where expert oversight adds the greatest value and where automation can proceed with confidence. Through this work, we aim to establish both the feasibility and the methodological boundaries of fully automated systematic review production.
